excerpt: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا ٱلذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَفِظُونَ
The people of interpretation say that every prophet is the man of his time because he is the founder of his time
source_location_hint: doctrinal formulation paralleling Kirmani's esoteric exegesis method

excerpt: O Ali and you are the parents of all the believers
source_authority: transmitted across Sunni and Shi'a sources
source_location_hint: widely cited from Musnad Ahmad and other collections

excerpt: the virtues of Abu Bakr, the virtues of Umar and the virtues of Uthman, and find the virtues of all of them equal to the virtues of Ali alone
source_location_hint: recorded debate of Ma'mun al-Rashid with Qadi Ishaq

excerpt: Gabriel came to him and thereby the Prophet came to know Islam
source_location_hint: based on Qur'an 26:193-194 regarding revelation descending on the Prophet's heart

excerpt: There were twenty-three (23) while the rest of the army of Islam had a total of forty (40) polytheists
source_location_hint: Battle of Badr casualty figures, cited in Ma'mun's disputation

excerpt: Those who stay in their homes and strive in the way of Allah with their lives and property cannot be equal. Allah has given superiority to those who strive with their lives and wealth over those who sit at home

A roasted bird was presented to the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him). At that moment, he prayed: O Allah, send to me the one whom You love most, so that he may join me in eating this bird. Then Ali came
source_location_hint: cited in Ma'mun's debate as ḥadīth al-ṭayr (hadith of the bird)

excerpt: O Ali, you are to me as Aaron was to Moses
source_authority: transmitted in Sunni and Shi'a sources; debated by Ma'mun
source_location_hint: Ghadir Khumm narration, fundamental in Islamic tradition

excerpt: Did not ʿAmir al-Muʾminīn embrace Islam before Abu Bakr? Even granting that ʿAlī was a minor—was not the Messenger of Allah himself fully mature?
source_work: Rahat al-ʿAql or similar Ismaili theological disputation
source_author: Imam Muhammad Baqir or transmitted tradition
source_location_hint: Early Ismaili polemical tradition regarding Abu Bakr's claims

excerpt: Though two were present in the cave, when Allah describes the revelation of the Scrolls, He mentions only one—We sent down the Scrolls upon him. Scripture descended upon the Prophet alone, not upon Abu Bakr.
source_work: Qurʾanic exegesis (taʾwīl) tradition; cf. al-Kirmani or al-Sijistani

excerpt: Allah declares that He sends down His tranquility only upon His Messenger or upon the believers. In that verse of the cave, Allah bestowed tranquility upon the Messenger—but not upon Abu Bakr. By divine justice, were Abu Bakr a believer, would not tranquility have descended upon him equally?
source_work: Kitab al-Yanabiʿ or Rahat al-ʿAql (al-Sijistani or al-Kirmani)

excerpt: When the Prophet displaced Abu Bakr, was Abu Bakr not leading the prayer to the general congregation, and the Prophet to Abu Bakr alone? Have you ever witnessed a prayer in which a single congregation has two prayer leaders?
source_work: Theological disputation tradition; al-Iqtisar or Daʿaʾim al-Islam
source_location_hint: Prayer leadership (imāma) in Islamic law and Ismaili doctrine

excerpt: Does not Allah forbid entry into any house without permission? Abu Bakr was placed in the Prophet's house. From whom was permission sought? If you apportion one-eighth of Aʿisha's inheritance—one-eighth of one-ninth of the house—there remains no space whatsoever for a grave.
source_work: Legal disputation; Daʿaʾim al-Islam or Taʾwil al-Daʿaʾim
source_author: Qadi al-Nuʿman or Ismaili juridical tradition
source_location_hint: Inheritance law (Qurʾan 4:11–12) and burial protocol in Islamic jurisprudence
